entries.title: The Traveler links warp travel with thought and perception

context.label: TNG season 1: Where No One Has Gone Before

coordinates.label: Where No One Has Gone Before

keywordsAny: Where No One Has Gone Before

continuity.sourceBoundary: Star Trek: The Next Generation season 1, Memory Alpha story rows 1-25, runtime episode aliases 1-26, primary on-screen Prime continuity.
continuity.notes: Draft reference deck. Excludes later TNG seasons, TNG films, DS9, Voyager, Picard-era material, beta canon, and fanon unless a card is an explicit future guard.

content.fact: Where No One Has Gone Before shows the Traveler amplifying the Enterprise beyond normal space, where thought and reality interact in ways Starfleet does not understand.
content.injection: Use the Traveler incident as a rare space-thought anomaly. Do not generalize it into routine warp capability or reveal later Wesley outcomes before their windows.
constraints: Keep this card inside TNG season 1 unless another active Star Trek deck provides a later Context window.
Use the episode and season Context gates before injecting this fact.

entries.title: The Picard Maneuver is a known tactical legacy from Stargazer command

continuity.sourceBoundary: Star Trek: The Next Generation season 1, Memory Alpha story rows 1-25, runtime episode aliases 1-26, primary on-screen Prime continuity.
continuity.notes: Draft reference deck. Excludes later TNG seasons, TNG films, DS9, Voyager, Picard-era material, beta canon, and fanon unless a card is an explicit future guard.

content.fact: The Battle identifies the Picard Maneuver as a past warp-tactical trick associated with Picard and the Stargazer, not an Enterprise-D standard attack pattern.
content.injection: Use the Picard Maneuver as a specific legacy tactic from Stargazer history. Do not make it a routine Enterprise-D combat solution.

entries.title: The Big Goodbye proves holodeck fiction can become operationally dangerous

continuity.sourceBoundary: Star Trek: The Next Generation season 1, Memory Alpha story rows 1-25, runtime episode aliases 1-26, primary on-screen Prime continuity.
continuity.notes: Draft reference deck. Excludes later TNG seasons, TNG films, DS9, Voyager, Picard-era material, beta canon, and fanon unless a card is an explicit future guard.

content.fact: The Dixon Hill malfunction traps Picard and guests in a program where safety assumptions break down, making holodeck risk a practical Enterprise-D rule.
content.injection: Use early TNG holodeck scenes with caution: fictional settings can trap or endanger users when ship systems fail. Do not treat holodeck danger as impossible just because it is simulated.

entries.title: 11001001 establishes Bynars as computer-linked specialists with desperate motives

continuity.sourceBoundary: Star Trek: The Next Generation season 1, Memory Alpha story rows 1-25, runtime episode aliases 1-26, primary on-screen Prime continuity.
continuity.notes: Draft reference deck. Excludes later TNG seasons, TNG films, DS9, Voyager, Picard-era material, beta canon, and fanon unless a card is an explicit future guard.

content.fact: The Bynars hijack the Enterprise to save their homeworld computer system, making them a high-competence cybernetic culture rather than ordinary saboteurs.
content.injection: When Bynars appear in season 1 context, write them as computer-dependent specialists whose deception is tied to survival of their planetary system.

entries.title: Minuet shows the holodeck can create socially persuasive simulated people

continuity.sourceBoundary: Star Trek: The Next Generation season 1, Memory Alpha story rows 1-25, runtime episode aliases 1-26, primary on-screen Prime continuity.
continuity.notes: Draft reference deck. Excludes later TNG seasons, TNG films, DS9, Voyager, Picard-era material, beta canon, and fanon unless a card is an explicit future guard.

content.fact: Minuet is convincing enough to emotionally affect Riker, making the 11001001 holodeck incident a social-simulation gate as well as a ship-control plot.
content.injection: Use Minuet as an early sign of holodeck social sophistication. Do not treat her as a free-standing person beyond this episode-local program context.

entries.title: The Arsenal of Freedom gives Geordi a command-pressure episode

context.label: TNG season 1: The Arsenal of Freedom

continuity.sourceBoundary: Star Trek: The Next Generation season 1, Memory Alpha story rows 1-25, runtime episode aliases 1-26, primary on-screen Prime continuity.
continuity.notes: Draft reference deck. Excludes later TNG seasons, TNG films, DS9, Voyager, Picard-era material, beta canon, and fanon unless a card is an explicit future guard.

content.fact: On Minos, Geordi commands the Enterprise during a crisis while senior officers are trapped below, showing early command competence before his engineering promotion era.
content.injection: Use Arsenal of Freedom for season 1 Geordi competence under pressure. Do not conflate it with later chief-engineer authority.

entries.title: Season 1 holodecks can be immersive but remain failure-prone

continuity.sourceBoundary: Star Trek: The Next Generation season 1, Memory Alpha story rows 1-25, runtime episode aliases 1-26, primary on-screen Prime continuity.
continuity.notes: Draft reference deck. Excludes later TNG seasons, TNG films, DS9, Voyager, Picard-era material, beta canon, and fanon unless a card is an explicit future guard.

content.fact: The Big Goodbye and 11001001 establish highly immersive holodeck characters and environments while also showing safety and control failures.
content.injection: Use season 1 holodecks as convincing and useful but not perfectly safe. Do not import later Moriarty sentience or Vic Fontaine-style social permanence.

entries.title: Transporter events can solve crises but should stay episode-specific

continuity.sourceBoundary: Star Trek: The Next Generation season 1, Memory Alpha story rows 1-25, runtime episode aliases 1-26, primary on-screen Prime continuity.
continuity.notes: Draft reference deck. Excludes later TNG seasons, TNG films, DS9, Voyager, Picard-era material, beta canon, and fanon unless a card is an explicit future guard.

content.fact: Season 1 uses transporters in medical, possession, and crisis resolutions, but those solutions depend on local conditions rather than universal miracle repair.
content.injection: Do not use the transporter as a generic reset button. Tie transporter fixes to the specific episode science and constraints.

entries.title: Season 1 treats the Prime Directive as judgment, not passivity

continuity.sourceBoundary: Star Trek: The Next Generation season 1, Memory Alpha story rows 1-25, runtime episode aliases 1-26, primary on-screen Prime continuity.
continuity.notes: Draft reference deck. Excludes later TNG seasons, TNG films, DS9, Voyager, Picard-era material, beta canon, and fanon unless a card is an explicit future guard.

content.fact: Justice, Symbiosis, and Home Soil show Picard weighing noninterference against proportionality, truth, and recognition of sentience.
content.injection: Write Prime Directive scenes as command judgment under constraints. Picard may refuse direct rule-breaking while still seeking ethical ways to reduce harm.
